original_abstract: This study addresses the topic of Polish–Moldovan relations between 1492 and 1538. The starting date is marked by the assumption of rule in the Polish state by Jan Olbracht, while the ending date is delineated by the fall of the Moldovan hospodar Petru Rareş and the deepening of Moldova's political subordination to Türkiye, which put an end to the Moldovan–Polish conflict over Pokuttya. Furthermore, the capture of Budjak by the Turks resulted in the establishment of a direct border with the Ottoman Empire. The author has analysed the relations with Moldova of successive Polish kings: Jan Olbracht, Alexander Jagiellon and Sigismund I the Old. Thanks to her access to unknown documents from the Levoča congress, the author has managed to present the importance of this congress in the planned expedition of Jan Olbracht in 1497. She has also emphasised the role of the Chodecki family in the Polish–Moldovan conflicts during the reigns of Alexander Jagiellon and Sigismund I the Old, and discussed the intricacies of Petru Rareş's policies towards Poland, Hungary and Türkiye. Based on a very extensive research query, including German, Austrian, Romanian, Moldovan, Hungarian, Polish and Russian archives and libraries, she has undertaken a study and description of Polish–Moldovan relations at the time. The conclusions of the queries shed new light on the topic presented. The aim was also to set these relations in an international context. The need for their in-depth geopolitical analysis arose from the fact that Moldova in the 15th and 16th centuries was a place of intersection of influences and interests of Poland, Hungary, Türkiye and the Grand Duchy of Moscow. Therefore, in order to properly understand and interpret Poland's relations with Moldova, it was necessary to analyse the geopolitical and political–legal situation of each side.
humanized_abstract: As a result, the study deals with the issues of Polish–Moldovan relations in the period from 1492 to 1538. The starting point is established as the accession to the Polish throne of John Alexander, while the end point as the fall of prince Peter Rareş and Moldova's increased subordination to the Ottomans, which led to an end of the conflict between the two powers over Pokuttya. The direct border between Poland and the Ottoman Empire was also established after the Turks had conquered Budjak. The author presents the relations of Moldova with the Polish throne of each of the successive rulers: John Alexander, Alexander, and finally Sigismund I the Old, the basis for which she uses, among others, the previously unknown sources referring to the Levoča congress which was to prepare the planned expedition of John Alexander to Moldova in 1497, and the function of the Chodecki family in the struggles over Pokuttya during the reigns of the last two rulers, as well as the policy of prince Peter Rareş, which is an attempt to assess the actions of the prince in relation to his main neighbours: Poland, Hungary and Türkiye. As a result of an extremely wide-ranging research into various sources including, among others, the archives and libraries in Germany, Austria, Romania, Moldova, Hungary, Poland and Russia, the study presents the results of the author's research into the state of Polish–Moldovan relations during this period, providing new answers to the issues raised. The necessity of making a more thorough geopolitical analysis of the mutual relations between Poland and Moldova in the 16th century was caused by the role played by Moldova as a crossroads of the interests of various countries and their alliances including Poland, Hungary, Türkiye and the Grand Duchy of Moscow. The adequate explanation of the mutual relations between Poland and Moldova therefore required an analysis of the geopolitical situation and the political conditions of both countries.
